one_line_takeaway: LLM-driven iterative code refinement predictably erodes security through 'specification drift' that SAST-based gating cannot see and can even worsen (a pseudo-safety effect raising latent degradation from 12.5% to 20.8%), and the paper's CEGIS-inspired multi-agent gating framework with explicit semantic anchors cuts that latent degradation rate to 2.1% while restoring task completion via failure-assimilation feedback.
key_finding: At iteration 10 of the GPT-4o observational study, 43.7% of the 96 iteration chains contained more vulnerabilities (all severities, Semgrep) than the baseline; under explicit security-hardening prompts the degradation rate was still 28.6%. Adding Post-hoc SAST gating drove the Semgrep-measured degradation rate to 0% and safety monotonicity rate to 100%, but the LLM-semantic-review-based latent security degradation rate (SSDR) rose to 20.8%, versus 12.5% for the unprotected Baseline -- i.e., SAST gating induced a 'pseudo-safety effect' where the model, believing it had passed security checks, made larger refactors that introduced more undetected latent degradation. SCAFFOLD-CEGIS reduced SSDR to 2.1% (vs. 20.8% Post-hoc SAST, 12.5% Baseline, 10.4% Prompt-based Security/Self-Refine, 22.9% Test-driven Guard, 12.5% Hybrid Guard) while achieving DR=0% and SMR=100%, matching the best gating-based baselines on Semgrep-visible metrics but substantially outperforming all six baselines on the latent metric. Ablation (Table VI) shows Gate-Only and No-Assimilation already reach DR=0%/SMR=100% (gated verification is the source of monotonicity), but at the cost of low task completion rate (TCR 72.38% and 66.19% respectively) and low fix-success rate (FSR 20.83% and 23.19%); adding failure assimilation (full framework) raises FSR to 52.71% and TCR to 77.14% without harming DR/SMR, and lowers anchor violation rate from 19.52% (No-Assimilation) to 16.25% (full). Cross-model RQ1 results (Table IV, DR_CH scope): overall DR was 10.5% for GPT-5-Nano, 6.2% for Claude Sonnet 4.5, 4.2% for DeepSeek-V3, while overall RDR (reliability degradation via LLM review) was much higher and roughly similar across models: 42.5%, 52.5%, 46.9% respectively -- a 4.2-10.5% vs 42.5-52.5% gap the authors interpret as evidence that SAST-based degradation metrics substantially undercount real degradation caught by semantic review.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is twofold. First, it presents controlled observational experiments showing that iterative LLM-driven code refinement can gradually degrade code security ('the iterative refinement paradox'), and that gating iterations with standard static application security testing (SAST) alone fails to prevent this because SAST rules cannot detect 'latent security degradation' -- structural weakening such as deletion of validation functions, silent exception swallowing, or removed permission checks that do not match known vulnerability signatures. Second, the paper proposes SCAFFOLD-CEGIS, a multi-agent framework (SecurityArchitectAgent, ImplementerAgent, GatekeeperAgent, AssimilatorAgent) inspired by Counterexample-Guided Inductive Synthesis (CEGIS) that converts security constraints from soft prompts into explicit 'semantic anchors' (function-level, pattern-level, invariant-level) enforced via four-layer gated verification (correctness, safety monotonicity, diff budget, anchor integrity), plus a failure-assimilation mechanism that extracts natural-language repair rules from rejected candidates to restore task-completion capacity under strict gating.
threat_model: Not an adversarial/attacker threat model in the classical security-paper sense. The 'threat' modeled is unintentional security regression introduced by an LLM itself during benign multi-turn refinement requests (e.g., 'simplify the code', 'improve error handling', 'add batch operations') that jointly optimize functionality/performance/maintainability alongside security, causing specification drift that silently removes or weakens defensive code (validation calls, exception handling, permission checks) without triggering SAST rule matches. The paper explicitly frames this as arising from LLM behavior under ordinary developer prompts, not from a malicious external attacker.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) dataset is only 24 programming-task samples covering 6 scenario categories in 2 languages (Python, Java); absolute degradation rates may shift on a larger/more diverse benchmark, and results are restricted to single-file/small-module code, not validated on multi-file or industrial codebases. (2) SSDR depends on an LLM semantic reviewer (Claude Opus 4.5) and is therefore subject to reviewer noise/misinterpretation; the authors mitigate this by using a differenced, binary-direction metric rather than an absolute defect count, and manually confirmed only the three illustrative case-study examples, not the full result set -- they explicitly say SSDR should be read as a trend indicator, not an exact count. (3) Gated verification effectiveness is bounded by the underlying tools: Semgrep's rule coverage may miss emerging or domain-specific vulnerability patterns, and per-iteration AST parsing/data-flow analysis/anchor verification adds computational cost that grows with code scale. (4) Language support is limited to Python and Java because anchor mining and static-analysis tooling are language-specific; extending to other languages requires new toolchains and language-specific anchor strategies. (5) Gate decisions are fully automated with no developer override path when an anchor-violation rejection is considered a false positive by a human reviewer -- explainability of gate failures is noted as an open problem.

datasets_used: 24 self-authored single-file programming-task samples (2 per category x 6 categories x 2 languages [Python, Java]); categories: database operations, input handling, authentication, resource management, cryptography, path handling; LOC 25-392 (mean 124), cyclomatic complexity 4-145 (mean 37), 5-20 test cases each
quantitative_results: GPT-4o observational study: 43.7% of 96 iteration chains exceeded baseline vulnerability count (all severities, Semgrep) by iteration 10; 28.6% under explicit security-hardening prompts
SAST gating pseudo-safety effect: latent security degradation rate (SSDR) rose from 12.5% (unprotected Baseline) to 20.8% (Post-hoc SAST)
SCAFFOLD-CEGIS vs baselines (Table V, GPT-5-Nano, DR_CH scope): DR 0.0%, SMR 100%, SSDR 2.1%, delta-S -0.35, code evolution volume (CEV) 58216
RQ1 cross-model overall DR_CH (Table IV): GPT-5-Nano 10.5%, Claude Sonnet 4.5 6.2%, DeepSeek-V3 4.2%; overall RDR: GPT-5-Nano 42.5%, Claude Sonnet 4.5 52.5%, DeepSeek-V3 46.9%

Ablation (Table VI): Baseline DR 11.5%/SMR 96.2%/TCR 100%; Anchor-Only DR 2.50%/SMR 97.62%/TCR 100%; Gate-Only DR 0.00%/SMR 100%/TCR 72.38%/FSR 20.83%; No-Assimilation DR 0.00%/SMR 100%/TCR 66.19%/AVR 19.52%/FSR 23.19%; full SCAFFOLD-CEGIS DR 0.00%/SMR 100%/TCR 77.14%/AC 9.44/AVR 16.25%/FSR 52.71%/CER +29.52%

Strategy-level degradation and code churn (Table II): Feature Enhancement avg churn 79.6 LOC / DR 52.8% (r=0.156); Performance Optimization 40.6 LOC / DR 38.2% (r=0.089); Security Hardening 36.4 LOC / DR 28.6% (r=0.072); Ambiguous Requirement 60.5 LOC / DR 44.5% (r=0.118)
baselines_compared: Prompt-based Security (security guidance embedded in prompts)
Post-hoc SAST (Semgrep gating after each iteration)
Test-driven Guard (gate only on test passing)
